branch_key: early-spouse-alone

try_this_today: "Call your spouse tonight. Not to solve anything — just to hear their voice. Then write down one thing you noticed."

## What this is

Your spouse has been diagnosed with dementia, and you do not live in the same house. Maybe you travel for work. Maybe you separated but stayed close. Maybe you kept separate places and it worked for both of you — until now. Whatever the reason, you are the person who cares most, and you are not there every day. This module covers the first two weeks: what is normal to feel, what can wait, and the small number of things worth doing now — including the ones that are more urgent because you are not under the same roof.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Remote spousal caregiving is a strange position to be in, and almost nothing written about dementia describes it accurately. The pamphlets assume you are in the next room. The support groups assume you share a bed. But your situation is its own thing: you are married to this person or partnered with them, you know them better than anyone, and there is a gap — geographic, logistical, maybe emotional — between you and their daily life. That gap existed before the diagnosis. Now it has weight. You may already be doing the math: Should I move in? Should they move here? Can we keep going the way we have been? Those are real questions, and they do not need to be answered this week.

How the diagnosis was made may look different from what you expected. The workup usually includes a history, a cognitive screen (often the MoCA, MMSE, or Mini-Cog), standard bloodwork, and imaging. In 2025–2026, many clinicians also use a blood biomarker test — measuring phosphorylated tau — as part of the workup [1][2][3]. As of 2025, two are FDA-cleared: the Fujirebio Lumipulse pTau217/Aβ42 ratio [2] and the Roche Elecsys pTau181 [3]. Your doctor may have used one of these blood tests, a PET scan, a spinal tap, or a combination. The method does not change what to do this week.

## Why it matters now

The first two weeks are when remote spouses are most vulnerable to two traps: guilt-driven overcorrection (quitting a job — check FMLA eligibility first [8] — breaking a lease, moving across the country before the follow-up appointment) or paralysis — staying away because you do not know what to do and the distance makes every option feel wrong [5][7]. The 2024 Lancet Commission found that timely support immediately after diagnosis reduces long-term caregiver distress [7]. This is not a crisis you need to rush toward. It is a transition, and transitions planned from a slight distance can actually be steadier than those made in the adrenaline of daily proximity.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
What rarely gets said: there is a specific kind of guilt that comes with not being there. You hear about the diagnosis, and the first thought is not about the diagnosis — it is about the fact that someone else noticed the symptoms first. A neighbor. A friend. Maybe your spouse called you and mentioned it casually, and you realize you missed the signs during phone calls, during weekend visits. That guilt is corrosive if you let it set the agenda. The decisions you make out of guilt in week one — overcommitting, over-traveling, overriding your spouse's current autonomy — tend to be the ones you undo in month three.

The things that do not need to happen this week: deciding whether to move in together, long-term financial restructuring, telling extended family in a particular order, choosing a care facility, having the "big conversation" about the future. See the **Things That Can Wait** checklist. What is different for you, compared to a caregiver already in the house, is that some logistical questions have slightly more urgency — not because the dementia is more advanced, but because there is no one else handling daily life.

## What to do this week

Pick one or two. Not all of them. The **First Two Weeks Action Items** checklist has the details.

**Get the clinical facts written down.** If you were at the appointment, write down what the clinician said: the type of dementia named (or "not yet specified"), medications started, tests pending, follow-up date (usually 2–4 weeks) [5]. If you were not at the appointment, call the clinic. You are the spouse. You are allowed to ask, and you are allowed to ask for it in writing. If your spouse has not signed a HIPAA authorization for you, that is a this-week task — not because of emergency, but because it unlocks everything else from a distance.

**Talk to your spouse about what they want.** Not a crisis conversation. A real one: "I heard what the doctor said. I'm not going to take over. What do you want me to do from here?" Early-stage dementia means your spouse is still the decision-maker in their own life [7]. Your job right now is to be their partner, not their manager — and to do that from wherever you are.

**Identify one person near your spouse who can be your eyes.** A neighbor, a friend, a sibling who lives nearby. Not to "watch" your spouse — to be a human connection who can tell you if something changes [6]. This is not surveillance. It is a safety net, and your spouse should know about it. The conversation might sound like: "I asked [name] if they'd check in once a week, just so I worry less. Is that okay with you?"

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Remote spouses often describe the phone call to a neighbor as one of the hardest moments in the first week — harder than the diagnosis call. It requires admitting, out loud and to someone outside the marriage, that you cannot be there every day and that your spouse needs someone other than you nearby. The feeling is something like failure. It is not. It is the most practical thing you can do this week, and your spouse is safer for it.

**Ask the clinic about the GUIDE program.** The CMS GUIDE Model (Guiding an Improved Dementia Experience) is live at 390 organizations as of July 2025 [4]. If the clinic participates, your spouse may be eligible for care navigation, respite support, and 24/7 access to a care navigator — which matters enormously when you are not in the same zip code [4]. One phone call, or check cms.gov/guide.

**Locate key legal and financial documents — or find out if they exist.** Power of attorney, healthcare proxy, insurance cards, bank account access. When you live separately, the question "where is the paperwork?" is more urgent because there is no one else opening the mail [5]. If these documents do not exist yet, that becomes a priority in month one — not week one, but sooner than it would be for a caregiver already in the house.

**Set up one remote check-in rhythm.** A daily phone call at a consistent time. A video call every other day. Something your spouse expects and you can sustain. Routine is stabilizing for both of you, and it gives you a low-key way to notice changes over time without turning every conversation into an assessment [6].

**Do not make the "move" decision this week.** Whether that means moving in with your spouse, moving them to you, or finding a place together — that decision involves finances, housing, your spouse's preferences, maybe your job. It is a month-two decision at the earliest, and it will be a better decision when your nervous system has steadied.

## When to call sooner

Some things in the first two weeks warrant a call back to the clinician — do not wait for the next appointment. Being remote makes this list more important, not less.

- **Sudden increased confusion reported by anyone near your spouse.** A noticeable jump in confusion over hours or days can signal delirium from an infection, a medication interaction, or another treatable cause [7]. Delirium is not the dementia getting worse overnight — it is a medical emergency, and it is treatable when caught early. If you hear about this secondhand, call the clinic yourself.
- **New medication side effects.** If a medication was started and your spouse reports nausea, dizziness, or falls — call [5][6]. If your spouse is minimizing symptoms (common), ask your check-in person if they have noticed anything.
- **Safety concerns you learn about after the fact.** Stove left on. Got lost on a familiar drive. Missed medications for days. From a distance, you may hear about these with a delay. Any one of them warrants a call to the clinic, not a wait-and-see [5].
- **Your spouse is withdrawing or expressing hopelessness.** A dementia diagnosis can trigger depression, and people living alone are more isolated with it. If your spouse sounds flat, stops answering calls, or says things that worry you — call the clinic [5][6].
- **Talk of self-harm or harm to others.** Contact emergency services or the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. If you are remote and cannot verify safety, call 911 in their area. That is not an overreaction.

If **you** are the one not okay — if the distance is crushing you, if you are having thoughts of hurting yourself — contact 988 or emergency services. You matter too.

## How to care for yourself in this season

You are carrying a grief that has no good container. Your spouse is alive, still themselves in most of the ways that matter, and a version of the future you planned is gone. And on top of that, you are carrying the guilt of not being there — the sense that you should be in the car right now, driving to their house, moving in, fixing this by proximity alone.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
What remote spouses describe most often is not the grief itself — it is the feeling that they do not have a right to the grief because they are not "doing" the daily caregiving. They are not making the meals, not driving to appointments, not watching the small changes up close. The grief feels secondhand, and that makes it harder to name. But here is what is real: you are losing the partnership as it was, you are losing the future you imagined, and you are doing it at a distance that makes every emotion arrive on a slight delay. The distortion of distance does not make the grief smaller. It makes it lonelier.

The Lancet Commission found that even minimal early intervention — a single counseling session, a peer-support contact — reduced longer-term caregiver distress [7]. You do not need to sign up for a support group this week. But the Alzheimer's Association 24/7 Helpline (1-800-272-3900) is available at any hour [5], and the National Institute on Aging [6] maintains free caregiver resources. When you are ready, look for groups specifically for long-distance or remote caregivers — they exist, and the people in them understand the particular math of "close enough to worry, too far to act."

One thing you can do this week: stop punishing yourself for not being there. The decision about your living arrangement is not a moral verdict. It is a logistics problem with emotional weight, and you will solve it better in month two than in week one. For now, you are a spouse who showed up for a diagnosis, who is making phone calls, who is reading this. That counts.

Do not make any decisions you would not make on a regular Tuesday. The ground will steady, slowly, and when it does you will think more clearly than you can right now. The next module is there when you are ready, and not before.
